behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration: the failure can occur at the handoff between git-change parsing and category aggregation (a rename token can be mis-mapped into add+delete buckets). Integration->System: real git rename detection/output (including rename heuristics and status formatting from a real git subprocess) is required; mocked change records can hide this exact failure. Rejected higher levels: behavior is deterministic categorization, with no AI/LLM output and no multi-agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary real git repo, commit an initial file, rename it, and run the CLI as a subprocess against that repo. Assert stdout report includes the file only in `renamed`, with `added` and `deleted` unchanged for that path.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration: crash risk typically appears when diff parsing/decoding output is handed from process wrapper to parser module. Integration->System: binary diff behavior depends on real git subprocess output and byte/content handling; stubs with clean text responses can miss decode/parse crashes. Rejected higher levels: no model-quality or non-deterministic AI dependency; no chained agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp git repo, commit a binary file change (e.g., image bytes), run the CLI subprocess, and assert process exit is successful and report includes binary file in the appropriate counted category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration: failure appears at interface boundary where git-history retrieval errors are translated into report output. Integration->System: the critical failure mode is real git behavior on unborn HEAD/no commits (non-zero exit, specific stderr/exit semantics) from a subprocess; mocks can incorrectly normalize this path. Rejected higher levels: deterministic control-flow handling only, no AI/LLM and no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repo with no commits, run the CLI subprocess, and assert successful exit plus an empty structured report on stdout (not traceback/error output).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration: mismatch can arise from module handoff between history collector and report counter logic. Integration->System: validating against the repository’s actual history requires real commits, real git revision traversal, and subprocess output fidelity; fabricated histories can miss traversal/flag/parsing discrepancies. Rejected higher levels: this is deterministic counting, not model-evaluated correctness and not an agent workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real git repo history with known adds/deletes/renames/modifies across several commits, run the CLI subprocess for the target range, and assert category totals exactly match the known ground truth from repository history.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Unit->Integration: divergence can happen at handoff where change-source selection (index vs working tree) is interpreted by reporting logic. Integration->System: staged vs unstaged is defined by real git index/worktree state and subprocess commands; in-process fakes often collapse these states and hide real mismatches. Rejected higher levels: no AI/LLM dependence and no sequential agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp git repo, create equivalent modifications once as unstaged and once staged (without commit), run the CLI subprocess for each mode, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured stdout output.
